item.title: Ontarians oppose Ford government freedom of information changes, poll finds
Canada is pitching its energy ambitions in Texas — can the oil and gas industry be convinced?
item.description: Though oil and gas industry executives at CERAWeek, a global energy conference in Houston, welcomed Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson’s enthusiasm, they still say they want to see considerable action before they can be sure Canada's federal government is serious about changing the country’s reputation as being reluctant to expand the energy sector, especially oil and natural gas.

item.title: Sponsored international travel by MPs drops to 15-year low outside of COVID-19 pandemic
item.description: Members of Parliament last year went on the fewest sponsored international trips in the last 15 years, excluding the height of the COVID-19 pandemic.

item.title: Toronto Star wins Canadian Hillman prize for investigative reporting
Platform doors on the TTC would save lives and money. Why does it not have them?
item.description: City council this week is being asked to commit to moving ahead with construction for a pilot project at multiple stations by the end of 2027.

item.title: The NDP ponders a leap with Avi Lewis
item.description: Their big wave came with practical steps, but bruised New Democrats may be in a mood for social democratic dreams

item.title: Today at Toronto city council: Bad landlords, Billy Bishop and TTC safety
Doug Ford and Mark Carney to expand HST rebate to all new home buyers
item.description: The 13 per cent harmonized sales tax break on new houses and condos will extend to everyone — not just first-time home buyers.

item.title: Toronto school boards fight to allow housing development on part of their land — a move some residents say is ‘outrageous’
item.description: TDSB, TCDSB challenging city council decision that excludes school properties from plan that would allow mid-rise developments along major corridors.

item.title: Global Affairs laying off highest-skilled diplomats, union says, as GAC sets targets
Ottawa rejects bid to prevent sharing information about foreign-born Canadians with provinces, other countries
item.description: The federal government notified the Senate that it will not agree to amendment to Bill C-12, the immigration and asylum bill

item.title: Ottawa quietly dissolves task force assigned to seek justice for victims of plane downed by Iran
item.description: The decision to end the task force was communicated to victims’ families in early March

item.title: UN committee criticizes Canada for not filling corporate human rights abuses watchdog role
item.description: Carney government has left the top ombudsman position vacant for 10 months

item.title: Minister pressed why just 1 Iranian official deported after 24 deemed part of terror group
item.description: Public Safety Minister Gary Anandasangaree faced pointed questions Tuesday about why the federal government has deported one Iranian official, despite longstanding concerns about how the regime operates in Canada and abroad.

item.title: Air Canada CEO summoned to Ottawa over English-only condolence video for LaGuardia crash
item.description: Air Canada's CEO has been summoned to Ottawa to explain why he spoke only one of the nation's official languages when he offered condolences for those killed and hurt in the collision at New York's LaGuardia Airport on Sunday.

item.title: India proposes trusted traveller program to ease access to Canada for Indian businesspeople
item.description: NEXUS-type program would also benefit business travellers from Canada who are seeking swifter access to India

item.title: Politics Insider: Panel finds abuse at all levels of Canadian sport
item.description: Canadian sport system is in urgent need of a fundamental overhaul, Future of Sport in Canada Commission says

item.title: One dead in Mississauga home explosion
Doug Ford accuses privacy commissioner of politicizing his changes to access to information law
Mark Carney invokes Christian values as top court told religious beliefs don’t belong in government
Liberals to debate age restrictions on social media, AI chatbots
Canadian sport system ‘underfunded and unsafe,’ commission urges Ottawa to step up
item.description: Canadian sport needs an overhaul, and it starts with the federal government.

item.title: Catholic priest in Oakville charged with sexual assault
Humber Polytechnic to begin layoffs after call for voluntary exits falls short
Ford government warns school boards to keep graduation ceremonies ‘apolitical’
Doug Ford says privacy commissioner opposing record restrictions ‘politically driven’
item.description: Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday he believes the information and privacy commissioner, who is speaking out about changes he is making to freedom-of-information laws, is 'politically driven.'

item.title: Trim OAS for higher income seniors? 73% says yes, new poll suggests
item.description: A new poll finds there is growing support for a proposal to lower an Old Age Security (OAS) threshold for some Canadians in order to help reduce the federal government's deficit.

item.title: Ford says to ‘stay tuned’ on HST changes for home-buyers
item.description: Ontario Premier Doug Ford says his government will unveil details Wednesday about potential changes around HST rules for homebuyers.

item.title: Canada discusses Keystone XL revival with Trump administration officials
item.description: Canadian and American government officials discussed a proposed revival of the canceled Keystone XL oil pipeline during a meeting in Houston, Tx. this week.

item.title: Terrebonne byelection to use adapted ballot due to high number of candidates
Carney government has no plans to give provinces more say in judicial appointments
item.description: But Industry Minister Mélanie Joly said that when it comes to Quebec, Ottawa should be receptive to new ideas.

item.title: Carney government and Alberta still have no deal as MOU deadline nears
Justice minister says federal government won’t use disallowance power
What’s at stake as Supreme Court hears challenge to Quebec’s Bill 21
item.description: From the use of the notwithstanding clause to state secularism to Quebec's unique place within Canada, constitutional law experts say the case will result in a major new precedent.

item.title: Students protest OSAP changes at Queen’s Park
item.description: Students and union members are expected to descend on Queen’s Park today for another large protest over the government’s changes to student assistance.

item.title: Quebec government within its rights to adopt Bill 21, lawyer argues
item.description: Lawyers for the Quebec government argued in Supreme Court today that the province was within its rights when it adopted its secularism law, known as Bill 21.

item.title: Ontario to seize ownership of Toronto Island Airport lands and declare it a special economic zone
item.description: Premier Doug Ford says the provincial government will be seizing ownership of city-owned lands at Toronto’s Billy Bishop Airport and declaring it a special economic zone, invoking new powers that will allow it to override environmental and other regulations.

item.title: Justice minister won't meet premiers' demand for 'sea change' to how judges are appointed
item.description: Justice Minister Sean Fraser says he has no plans to change how judges are appointed in Canada after four premiers wrote to Prime Minister Mark Carney asking for more of a say in the process.

item.title: Pride festivals seek federal $3M as corporations pull back support amid DEI backlash
Federal justice minister rejects premiers’ appeal for input on judicial appointments
item.description: Premiers of Ontario, Quebec, Saskatchewan and Alberta wrote to the Prime Minister with the request

item.title: To stop long ballot protests, MP committee calls on government to change candidacy rules
item.description: A House of Commons committee is calling on the government to make changes to federal election rules it says would make it harder for electoral reform advocates to stage long ballot protests.

item.title: Federal minimum wage increasing to $18.15 per hour
item.description: The federal minimum wage will rise by 40 cents next week, a scheduled increase that keeps the wage in line with inflation.

item.title: 'Fragmented, inconsistent, unsafe': Commission calls for sweeping change to sport in Canada
item.description: A commission studying the future of sport in Canada has called for sweeping change to the way sport is structured, after its work found “the widespread presence of maltreatment and abuse” in a poorly resourced and organized system.

item.title: Mark Carney calls for humility in politics at National Prayer Breakfast
item.description: Prime Minister gave remarks at the annual Christian event in Ottawa, which was also attended by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre

item.title: Liberals to debate use of ‘nuclear option’ against notwithstanding clause
Progress on Phoenix pay system backlog is ‘limited,’ auditor general finds
item.description: The federal government is running out of time to clear the backlog of public service pay transactions under the old Phoenix system, said Canada's auditor general.

item.title: Quebec’s secularism law threatens religious and equality rights, Supreme Court hears
Federal union denounces CRA decision to end tax drop boxes, calls for reversal
Canadians with ‘Havana syndrome’ outraged as Ottawa backs disputed report
item.description: Global News spoke with eight former diplomats who say they and their family members — including children — continue to suffer mysterious symptoms after incidence in Cuba years ago.

item.title: Manitoba government expands tax exemption on food, aims to cut deficit in new budget
item.description: Provincial sales tax will be removed from food and beverages sold in grocery stores on July 1

item.title: Canada’s veterans rehab program under fire as MPs question contractor, federal officials
Canada’s immigration bill may compromise rights, UN committee says
Doug Ford takes over Billy Bishop as Ottawa stays on sidelines amid Toronto pushback
Toronto council asked to oppose Doug Ford’s push to bring jets to Billy Bishop airport: ‘It’s our waterfront’
Doug Ford says he would handily win re-election as he defends keeping cellphone records secret
item.description: Ontario's premier brushes off criticism of a plan to exempt himself and cabinet ministers from access to information laws.

item.title: How RCMP spies infiltrated the 1970s Indigenous rights movement
item.description: A CBC Indigenous investigation has found the Cold War-era RCMP Security Service had a widespread and intrusive surveillance operation targeting at least 30 legitimate Indigenous political organizations and hundreds of Indigenous people.

item.title: Smith-Carney pipeline deal to miss early deadlines, premier says
item.description: The first set of deadlines included in the energy and climate deal struck between Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith are not expected to be met, showing early challenges with the agreement that includes pursuing a new export pipeline from Alberta to the West Coast.

item.title: Are Iranian 'sleeper cells' a threat to Canadians? Here’s what intelligence experts say
item.description: Rhetoric around Iranian sleeper cells has spiked since the onset of the U.S.-Israel war with Iran. Counter-intelligence experts and a former spy tell CBC News that while the regime may not use dormant agents in Canada, the threats of lone wolf attacks and 'criminal proxies' are real.

item.title: How the RCMP spied on Indigenous organizations — and how we broke the story
item.description: Today, we begin publishing a series of exclusive stories that expose a sweeping RCMP program of surveillance, disruption and infiltration of Indigenous organizations between 1968 and 1982. Though long suspected by Indigenous leaders across Canada, the scope and scale of the spy program has remained hidden behind walls of government secrecy, until now.

item.title: Future of once-heralded corporate watchdog remains under review, Ottawa says
item.description: Carney government won’t say whether budget cuts hit departments overseeing forced-labour laws

item.title: Nominee for Parliamentary Budget Officer says she will be ‘a one-term PBO’
item.description: Annette Ryan tells MPs she will provide high-quality, independent and relevant analysis

item.title: Politics Insider: Supreme Court hears arguments on Quebec’s secularism law
item.description: The top court will examine in detail the Charter’s notwithstanding clause in relation to its use by Quebec to shield Bill 21

item.title: The cost of this city project has more than tripled — and it’s now more than five years overdue. One Toronto councillor wants answers
What flags should fly at government buildings? Two Toronto councillors have clashing visions
Carney did raise human rights with Xi, government clarifies, admitting earlier ‘error’
item.description: Public servants correct information government tabled suggesting PM did not ‘proactively’ raise human rights or foreign interference during Beijing visit

item.title: Speaker says NDP motion can’t result in warrant for Ford’s phone records
item.description: MPPs are returning to Queen’s Park for the first time since rising for a break from the legislature before Christmas. Follow along here for live updates:

item.title: Canadian military members safe after reports of damage at UN peacekeeping camp
item.description: Canadian Armed Forces members deployed in the Middle East are safe after a missile was intercepted on Sunday near the Israel-Lebanon border, the defence minister's office says.

item.title: Liberals' fiscal watchdog nominee vows to hold government's 'feet to the fire'
item.description: The Liberal government's nominee to be the next fiscal watchdog insists she'll run an independent and non-partisan ship — even though her candidacy has created a political rift.

item.title: Ottawa’s foreign student cuts went far deeper than planned
item.description: The post Ottawa’s foreign student cuts went far deeper than planned appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: Doug Ford defends FOI changes, warns of enemies seeking government secrets
What to expect when Doug Ford brings the legislature back on Monday
Immigration Department not keeping up with demand for student visa probes: auditor general
item.description: Auditor General Karen Hogan says the immigration department did not investigate or follow up on a large number of "high-risk cases" that weaken the integrity of the international student program.

item.title: RCMP failed to recruit enough officers to meet operational needs: auditor general
item.description: The RCMP has not recruited enough new officers or effectively assigned its members to meet its operational needs — raising concerns about public safety across the country, according to a new report from Canada's auditor general.

item.title: Auditor-General flags weaknesses in IRCC’s anti-fraud controls for international student program
item.description: Department failed to investigate more than 145,000 students flagged as not complying with the terms of their permits, report says

item.title: Replacing Phoenix pay system will cost at least $4.2-billion, Auditor-General report says
item.description: Report finds federal efforts to eliminate backlog of pay complaints are falling short

item.title: What’s at stake in 3 upcoming byelections for the House of Commons
item.description: Three byelections are being held on April 13 and the results could have an impact on both the makeup of Parliament and how long it lasts.

item.title: Groups challenging Quebec's secularism law tell Supreme Court it sets a dangerous precedent
House of Commons returns as Iran war continues and audit reports due
item.description: Members of Parliament are back in the House of Commons today, where the government is expected to face tough questions about new auditor general reports and the war in Iran.

item.title: Opposition parties to pressure Ford government to reverse OSAP changes as legislature resumes
item.description: Opposition parties plan to lean on the Ford government to backtrack on contentious changes to student aid as MPPs return to Queen’s Park for the first time since December.

item.title: Canadians face ‘tsunami’ of transnational repression in coming years, cyber-research group says
item.description: Think tank cites rise of U.S. authoritarianism, AI and Carney’s disinterest in human rights

item.title: Queen's Park resumes sitting after 14-week break, straight into budget season
item.description: Ontario’s legislature is set to resume sitting Monday after a 14-week break that ended in a veritable deluge of news, partial proposals and headline-grabbing musings from Premier Doug Ford and his government.

item.title: Toronto Region Board of Trade offers road map to tackle city’s traffic problem
item.description: Toronto was ranked second in Canada for worst traffic in 2025, with an estimated four days of time lost due to traffic for commuters.

item.title: Public servant ‘scared’ to take government’s retirement offer due to issues with Phoenix pay system
item.description: Jennifer MacDougall was told she owes the government about $10,500 because of a mistake in her pay file

item.title: What’s the future of virtual health care in Ontario? Despite funding cutback, it may have taken root
Canadians abroad call for increased voter turnout ahead of by-elections
item.description: Canadians living overseas argue barriers to casting a ballot could be affecting election results

item.title: MPs to question federal budget watchdog nominee as role remains unfilled
item.description: The Office of the Parliamentary Budget Officer scrutinizes federal spending decisions and helps to cost out campaign proposals during elections.

item.title: Elizabeth Renzetti: Doug Ford’s tried everything to keep his secrets. Changing the law is his last refuge
Doug Ford’s crackdown on ticket scalpers draws warnings it could push prices higher
